questions.explanation: People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are a special resource. People are human resources.

questions.question: Choose the ultimate resource on the earth from the following:
questions.explanation: People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are the ultimate resource.

questions.question:
Which one of the following reasons is best suitable as to why human beings are called the ultimate(best) resource on the earth?

  1. Human beings can explore, develop and convert the natural resources into useful goods as per the abilities and demands
  2. Human beings are found everywhere on earth
  3. Human beings are Renewable resources
  4. The population of human beings in the world is ever-increasing
questions.explanation: Humans have the ability to transferring physical material into a valuable resource. People can make the best use of nature to create more resources when they have the knowledge, skill, and technology to do so. That is why human beings are the ultimate resource.

questions.question:
Pick out the option that best describes the term human resource development?

  1. Increasing the population of human beings.
  2. Increasing the natural resources so that there is a proportionate increase in human resources as well.
  3. Improving the quality of people's skills so that they are able to create more resources.
  4. Increase in technological development so as to increase the lifestyle of human beings.
questions.explanation: Education and health help in making people a valuable resource. Improving the quality of people’s skills so that they are able to create more resources is known as human resource development.

questions.question: Using resources carefully and giving them time to get renewed can be termed as ________.
questions.explanation: If we are not careful then even renewable resources can become very scarce and the non-renewable ones can definitely get exhausted. Hence, using resources carefully and giving them time to get renewed is called resource conservation.

questions.question: Name the term that can refer to balancing the need to use resources and also conserve them for the future.
questions.explanation: Sustainable development means carefully utilising resources so that besides meeting the requirements of the present, also takes care of future generations. In short, Balancing the need to use resources and also conserve them for the future is called sustainable development.

questions.question: Which one of the following is considered as the Golden R's of conservation?
questions.explanation: Reduce, Re-use, Recycle, Recover. These are the 4 'R's to help conserve resources.

questions.question: Identify the best example to conserve resources from the given list.

options.option: We should maximize the depletion of resources
We should focus on the indiscriminate use of resources
We should focus on Optimal use of resources

questions.explanation: For conserving resources we should not waste it and focus on Optimal use of resources.

questions.question: Resources that are drawn from Nature and used without much modification are called ________.
questions.explanation: The air we breathe, the water in our rivers and lakes, the soils, minerals all are natural resources.

questions.question: Which of these does not have economic worth but is valuable?
questions.explanation: Some resources have economic value, some do not. For example, metals may have an economic value, beautiful mountains may not. But both are important because both satisfy some human needs.

questions.question: The types of resources on basis of stock are:
questions.explanation: On the basis of stock natural resources can be broadly categorised into renewable and non-renewable resources.

questions.question: Which of the following is a non-renewable resource?
questions.explanation: Natural gas is a non-renewable source of energy.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of sustainable development?

options.option: Using coal and petroleum deposits at a low pace
Using coal and petroleum deposits at a fast pace
Ignoring the lights when they are switched on but not required

questions.explanation: Many trees are cut down to make paper. We should be very careful not to waste paper.

questions.question: The exclusive right over any idea or invention is called
questions.explanation: The exclusive right over any idea or invention is called patent.

questions.question: The basis of classification of resources are
questions.explanation: All of these. The basis of classification of resources are an origin, level of development and use, and stock and distribution.

questions.question: The value associated with resources is_____.
questions.explanation: The value associated with resources is economic value.

questions.question: The resources whose quantities are not known are called
questions.explanation: The resources whose quantities are not known are called potential resources.

subtopics.name: Conserving Resources

questions.question: Resources drawn from nature and used without much modification are called
questions.explanation: Resources drawn from nature and used without much modification are called natural resources.

questions.question: These resources are inorganic in nature and constitute minerals, rocks, soils, etc.
questions.explanation: Abiotic resources are inorganic in nature and constitute minerals, rocks, soils etc.

questions.question: Which one of the following does NOT make substance a resource?
questions.explanation: Value and utility make a substance a resource.

questions.question: Which one of the following is a human-made resource?
questions.explanation: Medicines to treat cancer.

questions.question: Complete the statement. Non-renewable resources are:
questions.explanation: Non-renewable Resources are those which have limited stock. I.e., non-renewable resources are present in limited quantity. They are exhaustible in nature, which can be ended when they are prone to large scale consumption.

chapters.name: Land Soil Water Natural Vegetation and Wildlife Resources

subtopics.name: Land

questions.question: Name the most important natural resource.
questions.explanation: The land is among the most important natural resources. It covers only about thirty percent of the total area of the earth’s surface and all parts of this small percentage are not habitable.

questions.question: What percentage of the total area of the earth's surface is covered by the most important natural resource Land?
questions.explanation: The land is among the most important natural resources. It covers only about thirty percent of the total area of the earth’s surface and all parts of this small percentage are not habitable.

questions.question: What percentage of the surface of the earth is occupied by Water as ice sheets and glaciers?
questions.explanation: 70 percent of this freshwater occurs as ice sheets and glaciers in Antarctica, Greenland, and the mountainous regions of the world.

questions.question:
A few reasons as to why there is an uneven distribution of the population in different parts of the world are given below. Pick out the most appropriate reason.

  1. Due to varied characteristics of land and climate.
  2. Due to the fact that land covers only about 30% of the total area.
  3. Due to the fact that water covers only about 70% of the total area.
  4. Due to the fact that earth is the only heavenly body in the universe.
questions.explanation: The uneven distribution of the population in different parts of the world is mainly due to varied characteristics of land and climate. The rugged topography, steep slopes of the mountains, low-lying areas susceptible to waterlogging, desert areas, thickly forested areas are normally sparsely populated or uninhabited.

questions.question:
Give the most appropriate reason as to why plains and river valleys are densely populated.

  1. Plains and rivers occupy 70% of the total inhabitable area.
  2. Presence of rivers is responsible for moderating temperature conditions.
  3. River water contains a large quantity of dissolved salts and hence suitable for consumption.
  4. Plains and rivers offer suitable land for agriculture.
questions.explanation: Plains and river valleys offer suitable land for agriculture. Hence, these are the densely populated areas of the world.

questions.question: Given below are some reasons as to why the rocky slopes of the mountains and low-lying areas are sparsely populated. Pick out the correct reason from the following:

options.option: These areas are not suitable for cultivation.
Clearing of the forest land in these areas has resulted in a reduction in the forest areas.
These areas are prone to flooding during rainy seasons.

questions.explanation: Rocky slopes of the mountains and low-lying areas are sparsely populated because during the rainy season accumulate to the low levels and these areas become prone to floods.

questions.question: The fresh water is continuously being renewed and recharged through the ________.
questions.explanation: Water can neither be added nor subtracted from the earth. Its total volume remains constant. Its abundance only seems to vary because it is in constant motion, cycling through the oceans, the air, the land, and back again, through the processes of evaporation, precipitation, and run-off. This is referred to as the ‘water cycle’.

questions.question: Classification of the land, based on soil fertility:
questions.explanation: Classification of the land based on soil fertility: Fertile Land and Barren Land

questions.question: Identify from the list given below, the different type of land-based on the ownership of the land:
questions.explanation:
Land can also be divided on the basis of ownership.

  1. Private land
  2. Community land

questions.question: Name the term that can be given to Land that is suitable for crop production.
questions.explanation: Arable land is the land capable of being ploughed and used to grow crops.

questions.question: Out of the given options, which one refers to the land used for grazing cattle?
questions.explanation: Pasture Land is the land covered with grass and other low plants suitable for grazing animals, especially cattle or sheep.

questions.question: From the given options, pick out the meaning of the term Fallow Land.

options.option: Land which can be cultivated, but has been left unused for the season

questions.explanation: Fallow land is the land ploughed and harrowed but left for a period without being sown in order to restore its fertility or to avoid surplus production.

questions.question:
Reading the following statement:
Land is used for various purposes like agriculture, forestry, mining, building houses, roads, setting up of industries etc.
Connect from the given list, the commonly used term with respect to the above sentence
questions.explanation: Land is used for different purposes such as agriculture, forestry, mining, building houses, roads and setting up of industries. This is commonly termed as Land use.

questions.question: A few physical factors that determine the use of land are given below. Pick out the one that is not a physical factor.
questions.explanation: The use of land is determined by physical factors such as topography, soil, climate, minerals, and availability of water.

questions.question:
Cite the most important reason as to why Land degradation, landslides, soil erosion, desertification, etc are major threats to the environment, from the following options.

  1. Because of the expansion of agriculture and construction activities.
  2. Because people and their demands are growing because the availability of land is not limited.
  3. Because of suitable, planned and scientific methods of cultivation.
  4. Because of the superior quality of land used for cultivation.
questions.explanation: Land degradation, landslides, soil erosion, desertification are the major threats to the environment because of the expansion of agriculture and constructional activities.

questions.question: What does the term Land degradation refer to?

options.option: Presence of rocks and minerals in the soil
Decline in the productivity of cultivated land

questions.explanation: Land degradation is the process of deterioration of soil or loss of fertility of the soil.

questions.question: Some important techniques to cope with Landslide are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.option: Construction of retention wall to stop the land from slipping
Hazard mapping locate areas prone to landslides

questions.explanation: An increase in the vegetation cover is an effective way to arrest landslide.

questions.question: Name the term that can be given to the various layers in the soil after it is cut out like a slice of cake.
questions.explanation: A soil horizon is a layer parallel to the soil surface. It is the various layers in the soil after it is cut out like a slice of cake.

subtopics.name: Soil

questions.question: The major factors of soil formation are the nature of parent rock and ________.
questions.explanation: The major factors of soil formation are the nature of the parent rock and climatic factors. Other factors are the topography, role of organic material, and time taken for the composition of soil formation.

questions.question: A few factors which lead to soil degradation are given below, pick out the one that is not a factor for soil degradation.
questions.explanation: Mulching is the methods of soil conservation.

questions.question: The bare ground between plants is covered with a layer of organic matter like straw to help retain soil moisture. Name this important method of soil conservation.
questions.explanation: Mulching: Bare ground between plants is covered with a layer of organic matter like straw. It helps to retain soil moisture.

questions.question: Trenches are made in front of the barriers developed with the help of stones or grass along the contour lines in the field. From the following list, choose the name given to this method of soil conservation.
questions.explanation: Contour Barriers: Stones, grass, soil are used to make barriers. Trenches are made in front of the barriers to collect water.

subtopics.name: Degradation of Soil and Conservation Measures

questions.question:
What does the term Rock Dams refer to?

  1. Farming on rocky terrains.
  2. Rocks are piled up to slow down the flow of water to prevent gullies and soil loss.
  3. Rows of trees planted across the rocky areas for soil conservation.
  4. Natural barriers are created on rocky areas to check the wind movement to protect the soil cover.
questions.explanation: Rock dam is one of the methods of soil conservation. Under this method, Rocks are piled up to slow down the flow of water. This prevents gullies and further soil loss.

questions.question: Given one important reason as to why in the Intercropping method of soil conservation, different crops are sown at different times.
questions.explanation: Under Intercropping method different crops are grown in alternate rows and are sown at different times to protect the soil from rain wash.

questions.question:
Based on the clues given below, guess Who am I?

  1. Three-fourths of the earth surface is covered by this
  2. An important renewable natural resource
  3. Though primarily used for drinking and washing, also used for productive purposes like irrigation, generation of electricity, etc
questions.explanation: Water is a vital renewable natural resource. Three-fourths of the earth’s surface is covered with water. Humans use huge amounts of water not only for drinking and washing but also in the process of production. Water for agriculture, industries, generating electricity.

questions.question:
Cite one good reason as to why Earth is called Water Planet.

  1. Due to the fact that water is a vital renewable resource.
  2. Due to the fact that water is responsible for moderate temperature conditions.
  3. Due to the fact that three fourth's of the earth's surface is covered with water.
  4. Due to the fact that water can neither be added nor subtracted from the earth.
questions.explanation: Water is a vital renewable natural resource. Three-fourths of the earth’s surface is covered with water. It is therefore appropriately called the ‘water planet’.

questions.question: Water can neither be added nor subtracted from the earth. It volume remains constant abundance may seem to vary because it is in constant motion, cycling through the oceans, the air, land, and back again, through the processes of evaporation, precipitation, and run-off. What is this entire process called?
questions.explanation: Freshwater is the most precious substance on earth. Water can neither be added nor subtracted from the earth. Its total volume remains constant. Its abundance only seems to vary because it is in constant motion, cycling through the oceans, the air, the land, and back again, through the processes of evaporation, precipitation, and run-off. This as you already know is referred to as the ‘water cycle’.

questions.question: Some important rivers of India are given below. Select the one that is not in India.
questions.explanation: The Volga is the longest river in Europe.

subtopics.name: Water

questions.question: A few important facts with respect to the Dams built across the rivers are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.option: Water stored in the dams cannot be used for drinking

questions.explanation: Dams are built not just for irrigation but for electricity generation, drinking, water supply for domestic and industrial uses, flood control, recreation, inland navigation, and fish breeding.

questions.question: Name the life-bearing layer of the earth.
questions.explanation: Natural vegetation and wildlife exist only in the narrow zone of contact between the lithosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere that we call the biosphere. In the biosphere living beings are interrelated and interdependent on each other for survival.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a major vegetation type of the world?
questions.explanation: The major vegetation types of the world are grouped as forests, grasslands, scrubs, and tundra.

questions.question:
Full form of CITES:

  1. The Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora
  2. The Convention on International Threatened Species of Wild Fauna and Flora
  3. The Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species
  4. The Community on International Trade in Endangered Species
questions.explanation: CITES (the Convention on International Trade in Endangered Species of Wild Fauna and Flora) is an international agreement (between governments) that aims to ensure that international trade in specimens of wild animals and plants does not threaten their survival. Roughly 5,000 species of animals and 28,000 species of plants are protected. Bears, dolphins, cacti, corals, orchids and aloes are some examples.

questions.question: Which of these resources covers about three-fourth's of the total surface of the earth?
questions.explanation: Water is a vital renewable natural resource. Three-fourth’s of the earth’s surface is covered with water.

questions.question: What are low-lying areas very susceptible to?
questions.explanation: In a low lying area, water gets accumulated and hence it causes frequent floods.

questions.question: Which of these physical features are best suited for agriculture?
questions.explanation: Plains and river valleys offer suitable land for agriculture. Hence, these are the densely populated areas of the world.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of community land?
questions.explanation: Community land is owned by the community for common uses like the collection of fodder, fruits, nuts, or medicinal herbs. Eg: The Sunder ban forests.

questions.question: What is the majority of land in India used for?
questions.explanation: In India, the percentage area in cropland (cultivation) is about 57%. Hence, the majority of land in India used for Cultivation.

subtopics.name: Natural Vegetation and Wildlife

questions.question: Due to what feature is ocean water unfit for human consumption?
questions.explanation: Oceans cover two-thirds of the earth’s surface and support a rich variety of plant and animal life. The ocean water is however saline and not fit for human consumption.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not a factor of soil formation?
questions.explanation: Soil texture.

questions.question: Which one of the following methods is most appropriate to check soil erosion on steep slopes?
questions.explanation: Terrace cultivation.

questions.question: Which one of the following is not in favour of the conservation of nature?

options.option: Switch off the bulb when not in use

questions.explanation: Dispose of polypacks after shopping.

subtopics.name: Farm System

questions.question:
From the given options, which one is the appropriate definition for the term Agriculture?

  1. The Process of preparing the land for the cultivation of crops, and also to rear livestock.
  2. The size of landholdings in an area.
  3. The temperature and the amount of rainfall required for certain crops to grow.
  4. The extraction and production of resources by nature.
questions.explanation: The word agriculture is derived from Latin words ager or Agri meaning soil and culture meaning, cultivation, or tilling of the soil. Agriculture is a primary activity. It includes growing crops, fruits, vegetables, flowers, and rearing of livestock.

questions.question: Breeding of fish in specially constructed tanks and ponds is known as:
questions.explanation: Fish farming or pisciculture involves raising fish commercially in tanks or enclosures such as fish ponds, usually for food.

questions.question: Name the type of economic activities.
questions.explanation: The transformation from a plant to a finished product involves three types of economic activities. These are primary, secondary, and tertiary activities. Primary activities include all those connected with the extraction and production of natural resources. Agriculture, fishing, and gathering are good examples. Secondary activities are concerned with the processing of these resources. Tertiary activities provide support to the primary and secondary sectors through services. Transport, trade, banking, insurance, and advertising are examples of tertiary activities.

questions.question: Name the type of activity that includes all those connected with the extraction and production of natural resources.
questions.explanation: Primary activities include all those connected with the extraction and production of natural resources. Agriculture, fishing, and gathering are good examples of primary activities.

questions.question: A few examples of primary activities are given below. Pick out the one that is not a primary activity.
questions.explanation: Secondary activities are concerned with the processing of resources. Manufacturing of steel, baking of bread, and weaving of cloth are examples of this activity.

questions.question: From the following, which one best describes the Secondary Economic activities?

options.option: Activities that help the primary and secondary activities
Activities involving the processing of natural resources
Activities involving a large human population yielding good turnover

questions.explanation:
There are three types of economic activities: primary, secondary and tertiary activities.

Primary activities include all those connected with the extraction and production of natural resources. For e.g. agriculture, fishing, and gathering. Secondary activities are concerned with the processing of these natural resources, like manufacturing of steel, baking of bread, and weaving of cloth are examples of this activity.

questions.question: Pick out the example of secondary economic activity from the given list of options.
questions.explanation: Secondary activities are concerned with the processing of resources. Manufacturing of steel, baking of bread, and weaving of cloth are examples of this activity.

questions.question: Banking, Insurance sectors, etc can be a good example of which of the following type of economic activity?
questions.explanation: Tertiary activities provide support to the primary and secondary sectors through services. Transport, trade, banking, insurance, and advertising are examples of tertiary activities.

questions.question: Pick out the approximate percentage of the world's population engaged in agriculture.
questions.explanation: The percentage of the world's population engaged in agriculture has decreased over time due to industrialization and urbanization. According to global estimates, approximately 25-30% of the labor force is engaged in agriculture. Among the given options, 30% is the closest approximation. Options like 50% or 70% overstate the current global figure, while 10% is too low.

questions.question: Name the term given to the land on which the crops are grown.
questions.explanation: Agriculture is a primary activity. It includes growing crops, fruits, vegetables, flowers, and rearing of livestock. Favourable topography of soil and climate is vital for agricultural activity. The land on which the crops are grown is known as arable land.

questions.question: Name the term given to the commercial rearing of silkworms.
questions.explanation: Sericulture is the commercial rearing of silkworms. It may supplement the income of the farmer.

questions.question: Name the term given to the Breeding of Fish in specially constructed tanks and ponds.
questions.explanation: Fish farming or pisciculture involves raising fish commercially in tanks or enclosures such as fish ponds, usually for food. It is the principal form of aquaculture, while other methods may fall under mariculture. Worldwide, the most important fish species produced in fish farming are carp, tilapia, salmon, and catfish.

questions.question: Name the term given to the Cultivation of grapes.
questions.explanation: Viticulture is the science, production, and study of grapes. It deals with the series of events that occur in the vineyard. It is a branch of the science of horticulture.

questions.question: Name the term given to Growing of Vegetables flowers and fruits for commercial use.
questions.explanation: Horticulture is the science and art of growing (plants) - fruits, vegetables, flowers, and any other cultivar. It also includes plant conservation, landscape restoration, soil management, landscape and garden design, construction, and maintenance.

questions.question: Agriculture or farming can be looked at as a system. Which one among the following is not an important input of agriculture?
questions.explanation: Agriculture or farming can be looked at as a system. The important inputs are seeds, fertilisers, machinery, and labour. Some of the operations involved are ploughing, sowing, irrigation, weeding, and harvesting. The outputs from the system include crops, wool, dairy, and poultry products.

questions.question: Which one among the following is not an important output of agriculture?
questions.explanation: Agriculture or farming can be looked at as a system. The important inputs are seeds, fertilisers, machinery, and labour. Some of the operations involved are ploughing, sowing, irrigation, weeding, and harvesting. The outputs from the system include crops, wool, dairy, and poultry products.

questions.question: What does the word agri mean?
questions.explanation: The word agriculture is derived from Latin words ager or agri meaning soil and culture meaning cultivation, or tilling of the soil.

questions.question: Which one from the following list is not a Human Input to the agriculture system?
questions.explanation: Agriculture or farming can be looked at as a system. The important inputs are seeds, fertilisers, machinery, and labour. Some of the operations involved are ploughing, sowing, irrigation, weeding, and harvesting. Rainfall, on the other hand, is a physical input.

questions.question: From the following list of inputs, mention the one that is not a natural input in the agriculture system.
questions.explanation: Pesticides are substances that are meant to control pests (including weeds). In general, a pesticide is a chemical or biological agent that deters, incapacitates, kills, or otherwise discourages pests.

questions.question: Name the type of farming practiced to meet the needs of the farmer's family?
questions.explanation: Subsistence Farming is practised to meet the needs of the farmer’s family. Traditionally, low levels of technology and household labour are used to produce on small output. Subsistence farming can be further classified as intensive subsistence and primitive subsistence farming.

questions.question:
Which one of the following best describes intensive subsistence agriculture?

  1. A plot of land is cleared by felling the trees and burning them in thickly populated forested areas.
  2. The farmer cultivates a small plot of land using simple tools and more labour.
  3. Crops are grown and animals are reared for sale in the market.
  4. Herdsmen move from place to place with their animals for fodder and water along defined routes.
questions.explanation: In intensive subsistence agriculture, the farmer cultivates a small plot of land using simple tools and more labour. Climate with a large number of days with sunshine and fertile soil permit growing of more than one crop annually on the same plot.

questions.question: A few statements about Intensive Subsistence agriculture are given below. Pick out the one that is not applicable.

options.option: Bright sunny days with and fertile soils aides in growing more than one crop annually
A plot of land is cleared by felling the trees and burning
It is prevalent in the monsoon regions of south, southeast, and east Asia

questions.explanation: In intensive subsistence agriculture, the farmer cultivates a small plot using simple tools and more labour. Climate with a large number of days with sunshine and fertile soil permits the growing of more than one crop annually on the same plot. Rice is the main crop. Other crops include wheat, maize, pulses, and oilseeds. Intensive subsistence agriculture is prevalent in the thickly populated areas of the monsoon regions of the south, southeast, and east Asia.

subtopics.name: Types of Farming

questions.question: Which is true regarding shifting cultivation:

options.option: The cultivator own the plot and stays there
It is also known as ‘slash and burn’ agriculture

questions.explanation: Shifting cultivation is practised in the thickly forested areas of the Amazon basin, tropical Africa, parts of Southeast Asia, and Northeast India. A plot of land is cleared by felling the trees and burning them. The ashes are then mixed with the soil and crops like maize, yam, potatoes, and cassava are grown. After the soil loses its fertility, the land is abandoned and the cultivator moves to a new plot. Shifting cultivation is also known as ‘slash and burn’ agriculture.

questions.question: This type of farming is practiced in the arid and semi-arid regions of Sahara, Central Asia, and Rajasthan, etc. Herdsmen move from place to place with their animals for fodder and water along defined routes due to climatic constraints and terrain. Which type of farming is being referred to?
questions.explanation: Nomadic herding is practised in the semi-arid and arid regions of Sahara, Central Asia, and some parts of India, like Rajasthan and Jammu and Kashmir. In this type of farming, herdsmen move from place to place with their animals for fodder and water, along with defined routes. This type of movement arises in response to climatic constraints and terrain. Sheep, camel, yak, and goats are most commonly reared. They provide milk, meat, wool, hides, and other products to the herders and their families.

questions.question: Which of the following animals is not reared by the nomadic herders?
questions.explanation: Nomadic herding is practised in the semi-arid and arid regions of Sahara, Central Asia, and some parts of India, like Rajasthan and Jammu and Kashmir. In this type of farming, herdsmen move from place to place with their animals for fodder and water, along with defined routes. Sheep, camel, yak and goats are most commonly reared. They provide milk, meat, wool, hides, and other products to the herders and their families.

questions.question: The main motive of this type of cultivation is profit. A large area of land and a huge amount of capital is used for cultivation with the latest technological advancement. Name the type of farming from the given list.
questions.explanation: In commercial farming crops are grown and animals are reared for sale in the market. The area cultivated and the amount of capital used is large. Most of the work is done by machines. Commercial farming includes commercial grain farming, mixed farming, and plantation agriculture. In commercial grain farming crops are grown for commercial purposes. In mixed farming, the land is used for growing food and fodder crops and rearing livestock. Plantations are a type of commercial farming where a single crop of tea, coffee, sugarcane, cashew, rubber, banana or cotton are grown.

questions.question: Name the crop that is also known as Paddy?
questions.explanation: Rice is the major food crop in the world. It is also known as paddy. It is the staple diet of the tropical and sub-tropical regions. Rice needs high temperatures, high humidity, and rainfall. It grows best in alluvial clayey soil, which can retain water. China leads in the production of rice followed by India, Japan, Sri Lanka, and Egypt.

questions.question: Name the country that is the leading producer of Rice in the world.
questions.explanation: Rice is the major food crop in the world. It is the staple diet of the tropical and sub-tropical regions. Rice needs high temperatures, high humidity, and rainfall. It grows best in alluvial clayey soil, which can retain water. China leads in the production of rice followed by India, Japan, Sri Lanka, and Egypt. In favourable climatic conditions as in West Bengal and Bangladesh two to three crops, a year are grown.

questions.question: Which among the following are fibre crops?
questions.explanation:
Cotton requires high temperature, light rainfall, two hundred and ten frost-free days, and bright sunshine for its growth. It grows best on black and alluvial soils. China, USA, India, Pakistan, Brazil, and Egypt are the leading producers of cotton. It is one of the main raw materials for the cotton textile industry.
Jute was also known as the ‘Golden Fibre’. It grows well on alluvial soil and requires high temperature, heavy rainfall, and humid climate. This crop is grown in tropical areas. India and Bangladesh are the leading producers of jute.

questions.question: The major raw material for the textile industry, India is a leading producer of this crop. Requires high temperature, light rainfall, two hundred and ten frost-free days, and bright sunshine for its growth, this grows best on black and alluvial soil. Name the crop.
questions.explanation: Cotton requires high temperature, light rainfall, two hundred and ten frost-free days, and bright sunshine for its growth. It grows best on black and alluvial soils. China, USA, India, Pakistan, Brazil, and Egypt are the leading producers of cotton. It is one of the main raw materials for the cotton textile industry.

questions.question: Which crop is also known as the Golden Fibre.
questions.explanation: Jute was also known as the ‘Golden Fibre’. It grows well on alluvial soil and requires high temperature, heavy rainfall, and humid climate. This crop is grown in tropical areas. India and Bangladesh are the leading producers of jute.

subtopics.name: Major Crops

questions.question: Coffee requires a warm and wet climate and well-drained loamy soil. Name the country that is the leading producer of Coffee.
questions.explanation: Coffee requires warm and wet climate and well-drained loamy soil. Hill slopes are more suitable for the growth of this crop. Brazil is the leading producer of coffee followed by Columbia and India.

questions.question: A few conditions with respect to the growth of Tea are given below. Pick out the one that is apt to Coffee.

options.option: Labour in large number is required to pick the leaves
Requires a warm and wet climate and well-drained loamy soil
Needs well-drained loamy soils and gentle slopes

questions.explanation: Coffee requires a warm and wet climate and well-drained loamy soil. Hill slopes are more suitable for the growth of this crop. Whereas, tea requires a cool climate and well-distributed high rainfall throughout the year for the growth of its tender leaves. It needs well-drained loamy soils and gentle slopes. Labour in large numbers is required to pick the leaves.

questions.question: The efforts made to increase farm production in order to meet the growing demand of the increasing population is called ________.
questions.explanation: Agricultural Development refers to efforts made to increase farm production in order to meet the growing demand of the increasing population. This can be achieved in many ways such as increasing the cropped area, the number of crops grown, improving irrigation facilities, the use of fertilisers and high yielding variety of seeds. The mechanization of agriculture is also another aspect of agricultural development.

questions.question: Which of these is a tertiary activity?
questions.explanation: Tertiary activities provide support to the primary and secondary sectors through services. Transport, trade, banking, insurance, and advertising are examples of tertiary activities.

questions.question: What is the breeding of fish?
questions.explanation: Pisciculture is the breeding of fish in specially constructed tanks and ponds.

questions.question: What is the main crop in intensive subsistence agriculture?
questions.explanation: In intensive subsistence agriculture, the farmer cultivates a small plot of land using simple tools and more labour. Climate with a large number of days with sunshine and fertile soil permit growing of more than one crop annually on the same plot. Rice is the main crop. Other crops include wheat, maize, pulses, and oilseeds. Intensive subsistence agriculture is prevalent in the thickly populated areas of the monsoon regions of the south, southeast, and east Asia.

questions.question: Which form of farming is also called slash and burn agriculture?
questions.explanation: Shifting cultivation is practised in the thickly forested areas of Amazon basin, tropical Africa, parts of Southeast Asia, and Northeast India. These are the areas of heavy rainfall and quick regeneration of vegetation. A plot of land is cleared by felling the trees and burning them. The ashes are then mixed with the soil and crops like maize, yam, potatoes, and cassava are grown. After the soil loses its fertility, the land is abandoned and the cultivator moves to a new plot. Shifting cultivation is also known as ‘slash and burn’ agriculture.

questions.question: Which of these is not a plantation product?
questions.explanation: Plantations are a type of commercial farming where the single crops of tea, coffee, sugarcane, cashew, rubber, banana, or cotton are grown. A large amount of labour and capital are required. The produce may be processed on the farm itself or in nearby factories. The development of a transport network is thus essential for such farming. Major plantations are found in the tropical regions of the world. Rubber in Malaysia, coffee in Brazil, tea in India, and Sri Lanka are some examples.

questions.question: In what season is wheat grown in India?
questions.explanation: Wheat requires moderate temperature and rainfall during the growing season and bright sunshine at the time of harvest. It thrives best in well-drained loamy soil. Wheat is grown extensively in the USA, Canada, Argentina, Russia, Ukraine, Australia and India. In India, it is grown in winter.

questions.question: Name the staple diet of tropical and sub-tropical regions.
questions.explanation: Rice is the major food crop of the world. It is the staple diet of the tropical and sub-tropical regions. Rice needs high temperature, high humidity, and rainfall. It grows best in alluvial clayey soil, which can retain water. China leads in the production of rice followed by India, Japan, Sri Lanka, and Egypt.

questions.question: Horticulture means

options.option: Growing vegetables, flowers and fruits for commercial use

questions.explanation: Growing vegetables, flowers and fruits for commercial use.

questions.question: Golden Fibre refers to
questions.explanation: Jute.

subtopics.name: Agricultural Development

questions.question: Leading producer of coffee is
questions.explanation: Brazil.

subtopics.name: Classification of Industries

questions.question: Which one among the following best describes an economic activity that is concerned with the production of goods, extraction of minerals, or the provision of services?
questions.explanation: It is because industries are the one which changes the raw material into the product of more value to the people.

questions.question: Which of the following can be considered as an example of the economic activity related to the production of goods from the industry sector?
questions.explanation: Because new goods are produced out of raw materials like iron, steel, etc.

questions.question: Which of the following can be an example of the economic activity related to the extraction of minerals from the industry sector?
questions.explanation: Because extracting the coal requires a process that is followed by the industries to extract the raw material.

questions.question: Which of the following can be considered as an example of the service provider economic activity from the industry sector?
questions.explanation: Banking falls under the tertiary sector of the economy and it provides banking and its allied services to the people in day to day life.

questions.question:
Which of the following best describes an agro-based industry?

  1. Industries depending on agricultural products Ex-plant and animal products.
  2. Industries depending on ores like Cement, iron, and steel, etc.
  3. Industries that use products from the sea and oceans as raw materials.
  4. Industries that depend upon the primary products from forests for their raw materials.
questions.explanation: Industries which derive its raw materials from animals and plant products are termed as agro-based industries.

questions.question: To which category of industry (based on raw materials) does Iron made from iron ore belong to?
questions.explanation: Because iron is metal which is obtained after extraction of iron ore(mineral) from the earth.

questions.question: Processing seafood and manufacturing fish oil can be considered as examples of:
questions.explanation: Because seafood and fish oil can be obtained in the areas with seas, oceans, etc., which supports marine life. Hence, it falls under Marine Based Industries.

questions.question: Pulp and Paper, Pharmaceuticals, Furniture, Buildings, etc are good examples of:
questions.explanation: Because the raw materials used in these industries are obtained from the forest region (dense trees). Hence it falls under Forest-Based Industries.

questions.question: Which industries are also known as a village or household industries?
questions.explanation: They are called so because they are small scale industries with less capital and hand made goods.

questions.question: Basket weaving, pottery, and other handicrafts are all examples of:
questions.explanation: Because, these goods do not require a large scale industry and a huge number of employees and moreover, these goods are hand-made.

questions.question: With limited capital, a food processing unit is set up in a particular village in Gujarat. This unit generates employment to that particular village. Name the type of classification of the industry under which this food processing unit will come under.
questions.explanation: It is because the industry is set up with limited capital and as mentioned the industry generates employment only to the people of that particular village.

questions.question:
This particular classification of the industry requires: 

  1. large amounts of capital,
  2. huge machines and modern and superior technology to manufacture goods,
  3. employing a huge number of employees. 

For eg: iron and steel industry, the production is on a mass scale and is voluminous. Mention the type of industry to which this classification belong to:
questions.explanation: As it is the classification based on the size, it includes Huge capital investment, more number of employees, and a high volume of production.

questions.question: ________ sector industries are owned, managed, and controlled by the state or central government.
questions.explanation: Because the public sector industries are owned and operated by the government.

questions.question: The industries like Indian railways, Hindustan Aeronautics Limited, etc., operate more for the benefit of the public rather than for making a profit and the price for their products is fixed through planning. Name the sector to which these industries belong to_______.
questions.explanation: Because these industries are owned and operated by the government.

questions.question: The leading industries of Birla Group, Reliance, Tata Group of industries belong to the private sector. Choose the characteristic features of these industries?

options.option: Operated mainly for the benefit of the public rather than profits, they are owned, managed, and controlled by a group of individuals. Prices of products are determined by market forces
Operated mainly for Profits, they are owned, managed and controlled by a group of individuals. Prices of products are determined by market forces.
Members of the cooperative society produce raw material and add value to earn a profit. Generally operates with the motive of community welfare
Jointly owned, they are owned, managed, and controlled by a group of individuals and government agencies. Prices of products are fixed through planning

questions.explanation: As the above-mentioned industries come under the ownership of the private sector industries owned by a group of private individuals. Therefore, such industries aim for profit-making and fix the prices based on market forces.

questions.question: Joint sector Industries are owned and operated by the state and individual or a group of individuals. From the given list of industries, which one can be called a Joint Sector industry?
questions.explanation: Because Maruti Udyog Limited is owned and operated by the state and individual or a group of individuals.

questions.question: An industrial system related to the Bakery industry is given below. Which of the following options gives the right data?

options.option:
INPUT: Bread
PROCESSING: Wheat flour, Power, Ghee, Oil
OUTPUT: Baking

INPUT: Baking
PROCESSING: Bread
OUTPUT: Wheat flour, Power, Ghee, Oil

INPUT: Wheat flour, Power, Ghee, Oil
PROCESSING: Baking
OUTPUT: Bread

INPUT: Wheat flour, Power, Ghee, Oil
PROCESSING: Bread
OUTPUT: Baking

questions.explanation: It is the process intending to produce bread as its final product. Hence, Wheat flour, Ghee, Oil, and Power are the inputs. The process involved in Baking and the output produced is bread.

questions.question: In the light of various factors that influence industrial location, some industries have a tendency to grow close to each other. Name such areas.
questions.explanation: Industrial regions emerge when a number of industries locate close to each other and share the benefits of their closeness. Major industrial regions of the world are eastern North America, western and central Europe, eastern Europe, and eastern Asia. India has several industrial regions like Mumbai-Pune cluster, Bangalore-Tamil Nadu region, Hugli region, Ahmedabad-Baroda region, Chottanagpur industrial belt Vishakhapatnam-Guntur belt, Gurgaon-Delhi-Meerut region and the Kollam-Thiruvanathapuram industrial cluster.

subtopics.name: Factors Affecting Location of Industries

questions.question: Name the three important industrial regions of the world.

options.option: Eastern North America, Western and Central Europe, Western Australia
Eastern Australia, Western and Central Europe, Eastern Asia
Eastern North America, Western and Central Europe, Eastern Asia

questions.explanation: Major industrial regions of the world are eastern North America, western and central Europe, eastern Europe, and eastern Asia.

questions.question: Which one among the following is not an industrial region of India?
questions.explanation: India has several industrial regions like the Mumbai-Pune cluster, Bangalore-Tamil Nadu region, Hugli region, Ahmedabad-Baroda region, Chottanagpur industrial belt, Vishakhapatnam-Guntur belt, Gurgaon-Delhi-Meerut region and the Kollam-Thiruvanathapuram industrial cluster.

questions.question: Name the important Western industrial region in India.
questions.explanation: Ahmedabad and Vadodara are the cities in Gujarat, which lies in western India and are a major industrial region.

questions.question: Name the important Northern industrial region in India.
questions.explanation: Because, Gurgaon, Delhi, and Meerut lie in the northern part of India.

questions.question: Which one among the following is an important industrial region of South India?
questions.explanation: Because this region witnessed the most rapid industrial growth in the post-Independence period. Bangalore is the capital of Karnataka and both Karnataka and Tamil Nadu lies in Southern India.

questions.question: Which industry is considered as the key or basic industry?
questions.explanation: Because, All industries whether heavy, medium, or light depend on it for the machinery. Thus, it provides the base to the other industries. Steel is required for manufacturing a variety of engineering, construction and defence goods.

questions.question: Which one among the following is termed as emerging industry?
questions.explanation: Because it is in the phase of growth and is expected to give vast exposure to the people globally.

questions.question: Emerging industries can also be named as:
questions.explanation: A sunrise industry is one that is new or relatively new, is growing fast and is expected to become important in the future.

subtopics.name: Distribution Of Major Industries

questions.question: From the list given below, which country does not have an iron and steel industry located?
questions.explanation: The countries in which the iron and steel industry is located are Germany, USA, China, Japan, and Russia.

questions.question: Many countries have a hub of the Textile industry. From the list given below, choose the country that does not have a textile industry?
questions.explanation: In Germany, Iron and Steel Industries are located.

questions.question:
Why is the iron and steel industry considered as a feeder industry?

  1. Usually, Iron and steel plants are located near coal mines.
  2. Products are used as raw materials for other industries.
  3. Usually, iron and steel plants are located where there is an abundant supply of water and power.
  4. Steel is tough and strong.
questions.explanation: Because its products are used as raw material for other industries. For example steel.

questions.question: Name the largest producer of steel in the world.
questions.explanation: China is the largest producer of steel in the world.

questions.question: Which place in the USA is called as the original centre of steel making and is slowly loosing ground and is now known as the dust bowl of America.
questions.explanation: Because today very few of the large steel mills are in Pittsburgh itself. They are located in the valleys of the Monogahela and Allegheny rivers above Pittsburgh and along the Ohio River below it.

questions.question: Name the important and the only iron and steel plant in India before 1947.
questions.explanation: Before 1947, there was only one iron and steel plant in the country - Tata Iron and Steel Company Limited (TISCO). It was privately owned. After Independence, the government took the initiative and set up several iron and steel plants. TISCO was started in 1907 at Sakchi, near the confluence of the rivers Subarnarekha and Kharkai in Jharkhand.

questions.question:
Why was Jamshedpur chosen to set up the steel plant? Pick out the most appropriate answer from the given options.

  1. Abundant iron ore deposits
  2. Abundant coal and manganese deposits
  3. Availability of limestone and dolomite
  4. Cheap labour
  5. Direct link to major markets and ports of Kolkatta and Mumbai
questions.explanation: Because this place was only 32 km away from Kalimati station on the Bengal-Nagpur railway line. It was close to the iron ore, coal, and manganese deposits as well as to Kolkata which provided a large market. TISCO gets coal from Jharia coalfields, and iron ore, limestone, dolomite, and manganese from Odisha and Chhattisgarh. The Kharkai and Subarnarekha rivers ensured sufficient water supply. Government initiatives provided adequate capital for its later development.

subtopics.name: Iron and Steel Industry

questions.question: In India, which city is considered to be the main hub of the IT industry?
questions.explanation: Because, Bengaluru has always had a unique advantage, as a city with the highest availability of middle and top management talent.

questions.question:
From where did the Silicon Valley get its name?

  1. Got its name from a valley by the same name that existed in California, US.
  2. Got its name from the meaning of the word silicon.
  3. Got its name from the particular computer software.
  4. Got its name from the silicon that was used to make chips for computers.
questions.explanation: Bengaluru is located on the Deccan Plateau from where it gets the name ‘Silicon Plateau’. The city is known for its mild climate throughout the year. Silicon Valley is a part of Santa Clara Valley, located next to the Rocky Mountains of North America.

questions.question: In 1980, the Government of Karnataka developed an electronic city in Bangalore, 18 km from the core city. Find out the name of that city.
questions.explanation: Because the city is known for its mild climate throughout the year. Silicon Valley is a part of Santa Clara Valley, located next to the Rocky Mountains of North America. The area has a temperate climate with the temperatures rarely dropping below 0 degrees centigrade.

questions.question: What class of economic activities does manufacturing come under?
questions.explanation: Secondary activities or manufacturing change raw materials into products of more value to people.

questions.question: Which industry is the base of all other industries?
questions.explanation: Iron and steel industry is the base of other industries. This is a feeder industry whose products are used as raw material for other industries.

questions.question: What class of industries does Maruti Udyog come under?
questions.explanation: Joint sector industries are owned and operated by the state and individuals or a group of individuals. Maruti Udyog Limited is an example of a joint sector industry.

questions.question: Which of these factors affect the location of industries?
questions.explanation: The factors affecting the location of industries are the availability of raw material, land, water, labour, power, capital, transport, and market.

questions.question: What process does the iron ore undergo in a blast furnace?
questions.explanation: The process of converting iron ore into steel involves many stages. The raw material is put in the blast furnace where it undergoes smelting.

questions.question: What is the output of the iron and steel industry?
questions.explanation: The process of converting iron ore into steel involves many stages. The raw material is put in the blast furnace where it undergoes smelting. It is then refined. The output obtained is steel which may be used by other industries as raw material.

questions.question: Which of these states has some major steel-producing centres?
questions.explanation: Jharkhand has some major steel-producing centres.

questions.question: What is the name of the place where TISCO began?
questions.explanation: TISCO was started in 1907 at Sakchi, near the confluence of the rivers Subarnarekha and Kharkai in Jharkhand.

questions.question: From where does the iron ore come to Pittsburgh?
questions.explanation: Some of the raw material such as coal is available locally, while the iron ore comes from the iron mines at Minnesota, about 1500 km from Pittsburgh.

subtopics.name: Distribution of Population

questions.question: Name the ultimate resource on the earth.
questions.explanation: People are a nation’s greatest resource. It is people with their demands and abilities that turn them into ‘resources’. Hence, human resources are the ultimate resource.

questions.question: On the basis of skill, labour employed in the process of production can be classified as:
questions.explanation:
On the basis of skill, we can classify labour (people) employed in the process of production into three groups:

  1. unskilled (no specific skill),
  2. skilled (has the basic skills required for the job)
  3. highly skilled (has expertise in the skills required for the job).

questions.question: Choose the option which is not the factor on which the productivity of a nation's human resources depends.
questions.explanation: People vary greatly in their age, sex, literacy level, health condition, occupation, and income level. The productivity of nation's human resources depends on these factors.

questions.question:
Pick out the appropriate definition for population distribution.

  1. The way in which people live in urban areas.
  2. The way in which people live in the villages.
  3. The way in which people are spread across the earth surface.
  4. The way in which skilled human resources are utilized in a country.
questions.explanation: The way in which people are spread across the earth's surface is known as the pattern of population distribution.

questions.question: Choose the percentage of the total population that lives in Asia.
questions.explanation: Of every 100 people in the world, 60 people live in Asia. Hence 60% of the world's population is found in Asia.

questions.question: The areas which usually enjoy an equable climate, adequate rainfall, where the land is flat and the soil is fertile are referred to as:
questions.explanation: The four major geographical factors which affect the distribution of population are a relief (shape and height of land), climate, availability of water, and resources like soil and minerals.

questions.question:
Population density refers to:

  1. the number of people living per square unit of area.
  2. the number of skilled people in a country.
  3. the way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. the number of unskilled people in a country.
questions.explanation: Population density is the number of people living in a unit area of the earth’s surface. It is normally expressed as per square km. The average density of population in the whole world is 45 persons per square km. South-Central Asia has the highest density of population followed by East and South East Asia. Average density of the population in India is 324 persons per square km.

questions.question: ________ percent of the world's people stay in just 10 countries.
questions.explanation: Sixty percent of the world’s people stay in just 10 countries. All of them have more than 100 million people.

subtopics.name: Density of Population

questions.question: What is the average density of population in the whole world?
questions.explanation: The average density of population in the whole world is 51 persons per square km. Population density is the number of people living in a unit area of the earth’s surface. It is normally expressed as per square km.

questions.question: Choose the non-physical factor that affects the distribution of population in a country.
questions.explanation:
Factors affecting the distribution of population
Geographical Factors

  1. Topography
  2. Climate
  3. Soil
  4. Water
  5. Minerals

Social, Cultural and Economic Factors

  1. Social
  2. Cultural
  3. Economic

subtopics.name: Factors Affecting Distribution of Population

questions.question: Give one example where countries with stable governments tend to have denser populations.
questions.explanation: Countries with stable governments tend to have a high population density e.g. Singapore. As people feel more secured and protected.

questions.question: This is an example of a place where religious or cultural activities tend to attract more people.
questions.explanation: Places with religion or cultural significance attract people. Varanasi, Jerusalem and Vatican city are some examples.

questions.question: Cite an example from the following which shows that people usually avoid extreme climates.
questions.explanation: People usually avoid extreme climates that are very cold like polar regions of Russia, Canada, and Antarctica.

questions.question: Give reasons as to why Alaska in North America is more populated.

options.option: Due to the availability of mineral resources
Due to the fertile soils suitable for agriculture

questions.explanation: Alaska is densely populated because of the availability of mineral resources and rapid industrial development, along with a suitable climate for settlement.

questions.question: Choose an appropriate reason from the following why Osaka in Japan and Mumbai in India are two densely populated areas.

options.option: These are areas with excellent climatic conditions.

questions.explanation: Industrial areas provide employment opportunities. A large number of people are attracted to these areas. Osaka in Japan and Mumbai in India are two densely populated areas.

questions.question:
Population change refers to:

  1. the change in the number of people living in a particular area.
  2. the number of people living per square unit of area.
  3. the way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. the age-sex composition of a country.
questions.explanation: Population change refers to change in the number of people during a specific time.

questions.question:
Pick out the appropriate definition for natural growth rate from the following options:

  1. The number of people living per square unit of area.
  2. The difference between the birth rate and the death rate of a country.
  3. The way in which people are spread across the earth's surface.
  4. The age-sex composition of a country.
questions.explanation: The difference between the birth rate and the death rate of a country is called the natural growth rate. The population increase in the world is mainly due to a rapid increase in the natural growth rate.

questions.question:
Life expectancy refers to:

  1. the number of years that an average person can expect to live.
  2. the number of literate people in the country.
  3. the number of working people in the country.
  4. the number of illiterate and uneducated people in the country.
questions.explanation: Life expectancy: It is the number of years that an average person can expect to live.

subtopics.name: Population Change

questions.question:
Birth rate refers to:

  1. the number of deaths per 1,000 people.
  2. the number of infants died per 1,000 people.
  3. the number of people moving from one place to another.
  4. the number of live births per 1,000 people.
questions.explanation: Birth rate refers to the number of live births per 1,000 people. Births are usually measured using the birth rate.

questions.question: The number of deaths per 1,000 people refers to:
questions.explanation: Deaths are usually measured using the death rate i.e. the number of deaths per 1,000 people.

questions.question: The movement of people in and out of an area is referred to as:
questions.explanation: Migration is referred to as the movement of the people within a country or between countries. Migration is another way by which population size changes.

questions.question:
What is the population pyramid?

  1. A graphical presentation of the age, sex composition of a population.
  2. When the population density of an area is so high that people live in tall buildings.
  3. The pattern of population distribution in large urban areas.
questions.explanation: A graphical presentation of the age, sex composition of a population.

questions.question: If the birth rate is high and the death rate is low in a country then the population pyramid will:
questions.explanation: In the population pyramid of a country, in which birth rate is high has a broader base and if the death rate is low it will not rapidly narrow towards the top but will be a little broader.

questions.question: The factors which influence population change are:
questions.explanation:
All of these.

The factors which influence population change are:

  1. birth rate.
  2. death rate.
  3. migration.

questions.question: The country which does not have a high growth rate of population is
questions.explanation: The country which does not have a high growth rate of population is New Zealand.

questions.question: How much population lives on 30% of the land surface?
questions.explanation: 90% of population lives on 30% of the land surface.

questions.question: Which of the following countries has slow growth rate of population.
questions.explanation: The United Kingdom has a slow growth rate of population.

subtopics.name: Patterns of Population Change

questions.question: The Ministry of Human Resource Development was established by the Government of India in the year.
questions.explanation: The Ministry of Human Resource Development was established by the Government of India in the year 1985.

questions.question: According to the population, what is India’s rank in the world?
questions.explanation: According to the population, India ranked 2nd in the world.

subtopics.name: Population Composition

questions.question: Human resources differ from one another in respect of
questions.explanation:
All of these. Human resources differ from one another in respect of:

  1. age.
  2. educational level.
  3. sex.

questions.question: Most of the people usually prefer to live in this region:
questions.explanation: Most people usually prefer to live in plains region.

questions.question:
What does the term population distribution refer to?

  1. How the population in a specified area changes over time.
  2. The number of people who die in relation to the number of people born in a specified area.
  3. The way in which people are spread across a given area.
questions.explanation: The way in which people are spread across a given area.

questions.question: Which are three main factors that cause population change?
questions.explanation: Births, deaths, and migration.

questions.question: In 1999, the world population reached:
questions.explanation: In 1804, the world’s population reached one billion. A hundred and fifty five years later, in 1959, the world’s population reached 3 billion. In 1999, 40 years later, the population doubled to 6 billion. In 1804, the world’s population reached one billion. A hundred and fifty five years later, in 1959, the world’s population reached 3 billion. In 1999, 40 years later, the population doubled to 6 billion.

subtopics.name: Uncategorized

questions.question: Set of fundamental rules and laws according to that a nation/country is governed:
questions.explanation: A constitution is a set of rules that guides how a country, state, or other political organization works. The constitution may tell what the branches of the government are, what powers they have, and how they work. It may also state the rights of citizens.

subtopics.name: Why Does a Country Need a Constitution?

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is not applicable with respect to the constitution of a country?

  1. Constitution grants the citizen to get right to equality with discrimination.
  2. Constitution provides laws to protect the minority.
  3. The rules and laws are specially created to suit the needs of a country.
  4. Constitution lays down certain principles that help in the decision making of a country.
questions.explanation: Because the Constitution of India guarantees Right to Equality: All persons are equal before the law. This means that all persons shall be equally protected by the laws of the country. It also states that no citizen can be discriminated against on the basis of their religion, caste, or sex. Every person has access to all public places including playgrounds, hotels, shops, etc. The State cannot discriminate against anyone in matters of employment. The practice of untouchability has also been abolished. Discrimination is not supported/encouraged in India.

questions.question:
Which of the following is an inappropriate statement regarding the importance of the Constitution in a country?

  1. A constitution denotes the nature of the political system of a country.
  2. A constitution is framed to protect the interests of the higher and influential section of the society.
  3. To have unity in a country as people belonging to different ethnic groups, language, religions, etc coexist in a country.
  4. A constitution gives guidelines to the government as well as to the citizens.
questions.explanation: Because the Constitution guarantees the rights of minorities against the majority.

questions.question: Name the President of the Constituent Assembly who appointed a drafting committee to frame the Constitution.
questions.explanation: Dr. Rajendra Prasad, President of the Constituent Assembly appointed a drafting committee to frame the Indian Constitution.

questions.question: On which date was the Indian Constitution passed by the Constituent Assembly?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26th November 1949 and came into force on 26th January 1950.

questions.question: On which date the Indian Constitution came into effect?
questions.explanation: The Constitution of India was adopted by the Constituent Assembly on 26th November 1949 and came into force on 26th January 1950.

questions.question:
A few key features of the Indian Constitution are given below. Choose the inappropriate one.

  1. Indian Constitution is one of the lengthiest constitutions of the world.
  2. Indian Constitution defines the powers of the Indian legislature, executive and judiciary.
  3. It also contains special provisions to safeguard the interests of the weaker sections of backward classes of the society.
  4. Indian constitution cannot be amended.
questions.explanation: Indian Constitution is both flexible and rigid in nature. Therefore, there are certain provisions in the constitution which can be changed by a simple majority of the parliament. It does not allow us to change its basic structure.

subtopics.name: The Indian Constitution: Key Features

questions.question: Name the procedure through which changes can be brought out in the Constitution.
questions.explanation: A constitutional amendment is a modification of the constitution of a nation or state. Amendments can add, remove, or update any part of the constitution except its basic structure. It is often used when it is better to change the document than to write a new one.

questions.question: What does the word Preamble mean?
questions.explanation: Preamble to the Constitution of India is a brief introductory statement.

questions.question: How many articles and schedules were present in the Indian Constitution when it was written?
questions.explanation: At present, there are 448 articles in 25 parts, 12 schedules in the Indian Constitution. As compared to when it was written it had, 395 articles in 22 parts and 8 schedules at the time of commencement.

questions.question: A sequence of events that happened in Nepal proves people's struggle for democracy. When did this struggle start?
questions.explanation: There was a people’s struggle in Nepal in 1990 that established democracy that lasted for 12 years until 2002.

questions.question: During which year did Nepal adopt an interim constitution?
questions.explanation: In 2007, Nepal adopted an interim Constitution.

questions.question:
What was the huge task that the members of the Constituent Assembly had before them?

  1. India was made up of different communities with different languages, religions, cultures, etc.
  2. The population of India was quite high.
  3. There were no proper architects to frame the Constitution.
  4. The members of the Constituent Assembly always fought each other.
questions.explanation:
The members of the Constituent Assembly had a huge task before them

  1. As the country was made up of different communities who spoke different languages, belonged to different religions, and had distinct cultures.
  2. Also, when the Constitution was being written, India was going through considerable turmoil. The partition of the country into India and Pakistan.

questions.question:
One of the key features of the India Constitution is Federalism. Which one among the following best describes Federalism.

  1. Free from external control.
  2. All religions are treated equally.
  3. The existence of more than one level of government in the country.
  4. The aims and objectives are enforceable in a court of law.
questions.explanation: This refers to the existence of more than one level of government in the country. In India, we have governments at the state level and at the centre. Panchayati Raj is the third tier of government.

questions.question:
Define the term Sovereign as referred to in the Indian Constitution.

  1. Equal opportunities to everyone in the country.
  2. No discrimination on the basis of religion.
  3. People of India govern themselves through their elected representatives.
  4. An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority.
questions.explanation: Sovereign: It refers to independent people and no longer governed by any external authority.

questions.question: What does the term Socialist refer to as per Indian Constitution?

options.option: Equal opportunities to every one  to make use of the resources of the country
No discrimination on the basis of religion
An independent India, nol longer governed by any external authority
People of India govern themselves throught heir elected representatives

questions.explanation: The term ‘Socialism’ in Indian Constitution seeks to establish a welfare State.It gives qual opportunities to every one to make use of the resources of the country

questions.question: With respect to the Indian Constitution, there is no discrimination on the basis of religion i.e. all religions are treated equally, Which one of the following terms refers to the above statement?
questions.explanation: A secular state is one in which the state does not officially promote any one religion as the state religion.

questions.question: Which term refers to All citizens of India have political rights which means that they can elect their representatives in the government with special reference to the Indian Constitution?
questions.explanation: A democratic country, government, or political system is governed by representatives who are elected by the people.

questions.question:
India is a Republic country. Choose the exact meaning of this statement from the options given below:

  1. The Indian Constitution proclaims that the head of the state will be elected and not be a hereditary ruler.
  2. Equal opportunities to everyone in the country.
  3. No discrimination on the basis of religion.
  4. An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority.
questions.explanation:
1. A state in which the supreme power rests in the body of citizens entitled to vote and is exercised by representatives chosen directly or indirectly by them.
  2. A state in which the head of government is not a monarch or other hereditary head of state.

questions.question: Reservations are given to those who belong to scheduled castes and tribes in educational institutions, government jobs, etc. This shows that the Indian Constitution has:

options.option: Citizens have the freedom to express their opinion
The Indian Constitution proclaims that the head of the state will be elected and not be a hereditary ruler
An independent India, no longer governed by any external authority
Strived to ensure an equitable and just society by reducing economic and social injustice

questions.explanation: The societal inequality in India is represented by the grossly inadequate representation of Scheduled Castes, Scheduled Tribes, and Other Backward Castes in employment and education due to historic, societal, and cultural reasons. The reservation is undertaken to address the historic oppression, inequality, and discrimination faced by those communities and to give these communities a place.

questions.question:
Fundamental rights refer to:

  1. The list of subjects of the central government.
  2. The list of subjects of the state government.
  3. The basic rights which are granted to citizens for the holistic growth of the individuals.
  4. The list of subjects of both the state and central governments.
questions.explanation: The Fundamental Rights, embodied in Part III of the Constitution, guarantee civil rights to all Indians and prevent the State from encroaching on individual liberty while simultaneously placing upon it an obligation to protect the citizens' rights from encroachment by society.

questions.question: The important fundamental rights to Equality is mentioned in which article of the constitution?
questions.explanation: Right to Equality is the first fundamental right assured to the people of India. Article 14-18 of the Constitution guarantees this right to every citizen of India.

questions.question: The Constitution states that all minorities, religious or linguistic, can set up their own educational institutions in order to preserve and develop their own culture. Choose the right which explains this.

options.option: Right to Cultural and Educational Rights

questions.explanation: Cultural and Educational Rights:The Constitution states that all minorities, religious or linguistic, can set up their own educational institutions in order to preserve and develop their own culture.

questions.question: The directives to the government which ensure social and economic reforms are called ________.
questions.explanation: Directive Principles of State Policy: This section was designed by the members of the Constituent Assembly to ensure greater social and economic reforms, and to serve as a guide to the independent Indian State to institute laws and policies that help reduce the poverty of the masses.

questions.question:
A few statements regarding the significance of fundamental rights are given below. Pick out the one that is inappropriate.
